Member-only story

6 min read
Just now

--

JavaScript has earned its reputation as one of the most approachable programming languages. You can declare variables without worrying too much about types, manipulate objects on the fly, and build complex applications with relatively little code. This flexibility is a major reason why JavaScript dominates both frontend and backend development.
But convenience comes with trade-offs.
Many of JavaScript’s “helpful” features save a few keystrokes today while quietly introducing bugs, performance issues, and maintenance headaches tomorrow. As applications grow, these conveniences can become liabilities that slow development instead of accelerating it.
Let’s explore the hidden costs of JavaScript’s most convenient features — and how experienced developers avoid their pitfalls.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

1. var: Convenience That Creates Confusion
When JavaScript was first introduced, var was the only way to declare variables. It seemed simple enough.
for (var i = 0; i < 3; i++) {
setTimeout(() => console.log(i), 100);
}
